title: The Biosynthesis of Chelidonic Acid
author_string: Malcolm, M. J., Gear, J. R.
abstract: A method was devised for the isolation of chelidonic acid from species of Narcissus and from Chelidonium majus, and for the degradation of the isolated chelidonic acid so as to isolate separable carbon atoms. Radioactive phenylalanine, tyrosine, pyruvate, aspartate, and acetate were poor precursors of chelidonic acid, indicating that it does not arise from aromatic amino acids, from members of the Krebs cycle, or via a polyketide intermediate. Radioactive glucose, ribose, and carbon dioxide were good precursors of chelidonic acid in flowering Chelidonium majus, and the distribution of activity indicated that chelidonic acid arises by the condensation of a three-carbon unit with a four-carbon unit. The feeding results further suggest that these units are produced from the photosynthetic reduction cycle of the plants.
